segments.s134-g97: بینداخت is a past tense verb (poetic form) meaning 'threw' or 'cast' and here comes first, followed by the agent الکوس and the object گرزی; چو کوه is a postpositive simile ('like a mountain') modifying the mace, intensifying its size or force.
segments.s134-g98: که introduces a result/relational clause ('so that' / 'that'); از بیم او is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/through fear of him'; شد is the past inchoative 'became' and takes the following predicate زواره ستوه ('Zovārah weary'), with the subject/name appearing after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s134-g99: به زین اندر is a locative phrase ('onto/on the saddle') with the preposition به preceding the noun; از زخم gives the cause ('from the wound'); بی‌توش is an adjectival/state expression and گشت is the past/inchoative 'became', so the clause means 'became bi-tosh because of the wound while on the saddle.'
segments.s134-g100: ز (poetic for 'from') marks source 'from the horse' and اندر افتاد is the verbal phrase 'fell down/was thrown off' with افتاد as the past of 'to fall'; و connects the clause to بیهوش گشت where بیهوش is 'unconscious' and گشت 'became' (inchoative), yielding 'fell from the horse and became unconscious.'
segments.s134-g101: فرود آمد is a compound expressing 'came down' or 'dismounted' (فرود ‚alighting‘ + آمد 'came'); الکوس is the agent; تنگ modifies the manner ('close, near') and از برش is a locative/positional phrase here meaning 'by his side' (poetic/elliptical use), so the clause means 'Alkūs alighted close by him.'
segments.s134-g102: همی + past verb خواست is a frequent epic construction meaning 'was about to' or 'attempted/wanted'; از تن بریدن is an infinitival phrase 'to cut/sever from the body' and سرش is the object 'his head' with the enclitic ش 'his', so the whole gives 'he sought/was about to sever his head from his body.'
segments.s134-g103: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; the clause places the subject رستم before the object برادر and the adverbial/phrase بران‌گونه ('in that manner') before the verb دید ('saw') — a typical poetic word order meaning 'when Rostam saw his brother thus.'
segments.s134-g104: به کردار آتش is a simile 'in the manner/character of fire' (کردار here = 'mode/way'); سوی او is the directional phrase 'toward him' and دوید is the past 'ran' or 'rushed', so the clause means 'he rushed toward him like fire.'
segments.s134-g121: This clause uses an instrumental phrase 'به نیزه' ('by/with a spear') followed by the personal name 'همیدون' as direct object and the prepositional phrase 'ز زین' ('from the saddle') indicating source. The verb 'برگرفت' is simple past (3rd person singular) 'took up / seized'; Persian often omits the explicit subject, so the action is presented impersonally/actively. Note the enclitic preposition 'ز' before 'زین' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from'.
segments.s134-g122: Word order here places the noun phrase 'دو لشکر بمانده' ('the two armies remaining') before the locative/experiential phrase 'بدو در شگفت'. 'بمانده' is a participial adjective meaning 'remaining/left'. 'بدو' (= به + او) means 'at him' or 'toward him' and functions as the prepositional object of surprise. 'در شگفت' is an idiomatic stative phrase 'in wonder/amazement' indicating the state of the armies.
segments.s134-g123: The verb 'زدش' is 'زد' (hit/struck) with the enclitic third-person object '-ش' = 'him', so 'he struck him'. 'بر زمین' ('to/onto the ground') gives the direction. The simile 'همچو یک لخت کوه' ('like a single/bare mountain') modifies the manner of the blow; note the adjective 'لخت' ('bare/plain') directly precedes 'کوه' ('mountain').
segments.s134-g124: This is a verbal clause with the copular/auxiliary past 'شد' ('became') taking the subject phrase 'جان توران گروه' (literally 'the lives/souls of the Turan troop'). 'پر از بیم' ('full of fear') is a predicative phrase: 'پر' = 'full (of)', 'از' = 'of/from', 'بیم' = 'fear'. Word order puts the predicate before the auxiliary 'شد' in this poetic line.
segments.s134-g125: Here 'برین' is the fused form of 'بر این' ('upon these / on this/these'), followed by 'همنشان' ('companions/peers' — literally 'those of the same seat/status'). 'هفت گرد دلیر' is a compound noun phrase: 'هفت' numerically 'seven' modifying 'گرد' (used here as a collective/warrior element) and 'دلیر' ('brave') describes them. Poetic Persian often stacks modifiers after the head noun.
segments.s134-g126: 'کشیدند' is past plural 'they drew' (here 'drew weapons'); 'شمشیر' is object 'swords'. The following 'برسان شیر' indicates the direction/target: 'برسان' (from the listed senses 'bring/deliver') functions here as 'against/toward' (i.e., bringing the swords upon); 'شیر' ('lion') serves as the target. In early/poetic Persian these verbs and prepositional complements can be compacted, so the sense is 'they drew swords against the lion.'
segments.s134-g127: The temporal adverb 'پس' = 'then' opens the clause. 'پشت ایشان' ('at/behind their backs') gives the locative; 'ایشان' = 'they/them'. 'دلاور سران' is a nominal phrase 'the valiant chiefs/leaders' with the adjective 'دلاور' qualifying 'سران' (leaders). Poetic order places modifiers in flexible positions, so the group performing the action (the valiant leaders) is foregrounded here.
segments.s134-g128: The verb 'نهادند' is past plural 'they placed/put'. The prepositional phrase 'بر کتف' ('on (their) shoulder(s)') shows the locus where the object is set, and 'گرز گران' is a noun phrase 'heavy maces' ('گرز' = mace, 'گران' = heavy/weighty) that functions as the direct object. Together: 'they placed heavy maces on (their) shoulders.'
segments.s134-g81: برون‌رفت is a past/perfective verbal noun/verb meaning 'went out' or 'issued forth' and here heads the clause (verb before the rest, a common poetic order). با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him' and ز لشکر سوار is a genitive/prepositional group 'from the army of horsemen' that completes where/from whom they issued.
segments.s134-g82: ز مردان جنگی is a partitive phrase 'of/among the warlike men' and فزون از هزار quantitatively modifies it: 'more than a thousand' (فزون از = 'more than'); the number phrase follows the noun phrase, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s134-g83: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; با سنان 'with spears' indicates the weapon used, and سرافشان شد(ند) is a stative/resultative expression formed with سرافشان (lit. 'scatterers of heads/with spears aloft') plus the past plural شد(ند) 'they became/they were', here meaning 'they raised/brandished (their) spears'.
segments.s134-g84: This is a coordinated list of arms introduced to continue the description started in the previous segment: (ابا) جوشن و گرز و خفتان شدند — شد(ند) is again the 3rd pl. past 'they took/put on/were equipped'. ابا here functions as a poetic variant/introducer equivalent to 'with' (با), and the nouns جوشن, گرز, خفتان enumerate the armor and weapons they put on or took up.
segments.s134-g85: زواره is a proper name (here a person). پدیدار functions as 'appeared'/'came into view' and is used predicatively; بد جنگجوی is an epithet 'a bad/fierce warrior' (بد here = 'ill/ferocious' in sense of hostile), modifying the appearing figure.
segments.s134-g86: بدو 'to him/toward him' is a directional dative; تیز الکوس is a noun phrase with adjective before the name 'the swift/keen Alkūs' (poetic adjective order). بنهاد is past 3rd sing. 'he set/placed' and with روی 'face' yields the idiom 'he turned/put his face toward him' = 'he faced him' or 'directed his attention/attack toward him.'
segments.s134-g87: گمانی چنان برد is an idiom: گمانی 'a suspicion/guess' + چنان 'such' + برد (here 'took' or 'held') = 'he formed such a guess.' کو in classical Persian is a connective meaning 'that' (introducing the content of the suspicion) and رستم‌ست = 'he is Rostam' (proper name with the copula -ست attached).
segments.s134-g88: بدانست is the past 3rd-singular 'he knew/realized'; کز is the subordinating conjunction 'that/from which' here 'that'; تخمه نیرم‌ست is a predicative phrase 'he is of the seed/offspring of Nīram' (تخمه = stock/offspring, نیرم‌ a personal/lineage name plus -ست = 'is').
segments.s134-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آتش is the subject 'fire'. بیامد is the classical past form of آمدن ('came'); the verb precedes the following prepositional phrase. بر + پیلتن is a prepositional phrase 'upon Piltan' (پیلتن is a place/name). Together: 'When fire came upon Piltan...'.
segments.s134-g58: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from which' (linking back to the previous clause). بود is the past copula 'was/there was'. نیروی + (اتِ) is the izāfe construction meaning 'the force/power of ...' and here governs the following coordinated nouns جنگ و شکن ('war' and 'breaker' — i.e. force for battle and for breaking). The whole clause means 'from which was the force of war and of breaking.'
segments.s134-g59: تهمتن is the proper/name or epithet (subject). به لبها is the goal/locative 'to (his) lips'. برآورده is a past or participial form of برآوردن used here in the sense 'having brought up/raised', and کف is the object 'palm'. Classical poetic word order places the participle and noun elements in inverted order: 'Tahmatan, having raised the palm to his lips...'.
segments.s134-g60: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. گفتی here introduces reported speech ('you said'); که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause. بستد is the 3rd-person classical form of بستن used in reported/future sense 'would/should close' or 'closed'. ز خورشید تف is a prepositional phrase 'the spit/from the sun' functioning as the object of بستد. Overall: 'you said that the spit of the sun would be stopped.'
segments.s134-g61: برانگیخت is a past tense verb 'roused/raised' (3rd sg). اسپ is its object 'horse/steed' (verb precedes object in this poetic order). و 'and' links to the next clause. برآمد is another past/occurrence verb 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is its noun 'cry/roar' (again verb + noun ordering).
segments.s134-g62: بران سان is an emphatic locution 'in that manner / so', where بران = 'upon that' and سان = 'like/so'. که introduces the result clause 'that ...'. دریا is the subject 'the sea'. برآید is a present/subjunctive or potential form 'rises/appears' and بجوش is a verbal complement 'to boil/seethe' (imperative/subjunctive form used in a resultive sense). Together: 'so that the sea seemed to rise up seething.'
segments.s134-g63: This is a pair of noun phrases describing equipment: سپر بر سر = 'a shield on (his) head' (سپر 'shield' + بر 'on' + سر 'head'). و links the second phrase. تیغ هندی is 'an Indian sword' (تیغ 'sword' modified by the adjective/postmodifier هندی). به مشت here is instrumental/locative 'in his fist' (به + مشت). The sequence uses poetic compactness without pronominal possessives, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s134-g64: ازان is 'from those' (referring back to the famed men). نامداران is the plural 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. دو بهره literally 'two shares/portions' is an idiom meaning 'two at once' or 'two persons' here. بکشت is an archaic past verb form meaning 'he struck/killed' (verb at the end follows the object phrases as SOV). The whole clause: 'he slew two of those renowned men.'
segments.s134-g9: بشد is an archaic/narrative past meaning “it happened / they advanced” (here narrative past). تازیان is the plural noun “the Arabs” and is the subject. تا + سر پل دمان is a positional phrase: تا = “as far as / until,” سر = “top/head,” پل = “bridge,” and دمان = “bank / foothill”; together they mean “up to the head (end) of the bridge’s bank.” Word order is Subject–Verb–until-phrase, typical of older Persian narrative compression.
segments.s134-g10: This clause describes how they were armed/ready: به here introduces the complement (roughly “with / on”), زه = “bowstring,” بر + نهاده is a compound verb meaning “placed/sett (upon)” (بر = “on/upon”, نهاده = past participle ‘placed’). دو زاغ کمان is a noun phrase “two (دو) black (زاغ) bows (کمان)” — i.e. two black bows strung on the bowstring. The order is instrumental/adverbial (به زه) then the verb (بر نهاده) then the object (دو زاغ کمان).
segments.s134-g11: چنین = “thus/so”; تا = “until”; به نزدیکی پل = a locative phrase where به = “to/at,” نزدیکی = “nearness/close by,” پل = “bridge.” رسید is the past tense of رسیدن “to reach/arrive.” Together this clause means “thus (they) reached up to near the bridge.” The verb is simple past and the clause is a temporal/location result of the preceding action.
segments.s134-g12: چو آمد is a temporal clause meaning “when he came” (چو = “when,” آمد = past “came”). درفش جفا پیشه is a noun phrase: درفش = “banner/standard,” جفا = “cruelty/injustice” used attributively, and پیشه = “practice/profession” forming the compound جفا‌پیشه = “cruel-minded / practicing cruelty.” دید is past “saw.” The sequence is temporal clause → object phrase → main verb “saw.”
segments.s134-g13: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (“for / that”). بگذشته بود is a perfect/pluperfect construction: بگذشته (having passed) + بود (past auxiliary) = “had passed/crossed.” او = “he.” ازین روی آب = a fixed locative phrase: ازین = “from this,” روی = “side/surface,” آب = “water,” so ازین روی آب = “to/from this side of the water” (here meaning ‘to this bank’). The clause gives the explanatory circumstance (he had already crossed).
segments.s134-g14: به پیش سپاه اندر افراسیاب is a directional/positional phrase: به پیش = “into the presence of / before,” سپاه = “army,” اندر = “in/among,” افراسیاب = the name Afrasiab. The whole means “into the presence of Afrasiab’s army” — an indication of where he had come relative to the host.
segments.s134-g15: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmatan, “the mighty one”) serving as the subject. بپوشید is past tense (he put on / donned) from پوشیدن; here it governs the direct object that follows. ببر is the noun “tiger” (used here as part of the compound meaning a tiger-skin) and بیان completes the noun phrase (a name/label or descriptor linked to the garment). The sequence is Subject–Verb–object (garment), an older poetic compression where two nouns form the object of بپوشید.
segments.s134-g16: نشست = past “sat down” (here ‘sat upon’). از بر is a slightly archaic locative/ablative combination where از + بر together indicate position ‘on/top of’ (in older usage بر can mark the surface and از can mark motion or separation); together they introduce the place. ژنده = “ragged/tattered,” پیل = “elephant,” ژیان = “life” (used figuratively of a living/huge creature). The clause means “he sat (down) on the ragged elephant.”
segments.s134-g41: Word order is poetic: the preposition ز (from/of) plus its object پیران (the elders) appears before the verb بپرسید (asked), while the subject افراسیاب (Afrasiab) comes at the end. بپرسید is a classical past (3rd-person) form meaning ‘he asked’ and the whole segment means ‘Afrasiab asked of the elders’.
segments.s134-g42: که introduces an indirect question/clause (‘whether/that’). این دشت is the subject phrase ‘this plain’. رزم‌ست‌گر is a compressed/poetic expression: ست is the archaic form of است (‘is’) and گر functions like an archaic connective (here opposing alternatives), so the phrase contrasts ‘is (a) battlefield’ with the following جای خواب (‘a place for sleep’). جای خواب is a noun phrase ‘place of sleep’ = ‘place to sleep’.
segments.s134-g43: که again introduces the clause. در رزم جستن is a gerund/infinitival phrase ‘in seeking/going to battle’ (در + noun + infinitive جستن). دلیران is the object/accusative ‘brave men’ encountered. بدیم here appears as an archaic past-form used with first-person plural (‘we …’); its exact orthography is irregular in the manuscript tradition, so it functions as a past verb meaning roughly ‘we saw/encountered’ (see note).
segments.s134-g44: سگالش is a rare/uncertain term (likely a proper name or a technical/object word in this passage) and functions as the object of گرفتیم. گرفتیم is past 1st-person-plural ‘we seized/took’. و links the clauses, and شیران بیـدیم (with the same irregular past form بدیم) repeats the narrative ‘and we saw/there were lions’ — here شیران is literal ‘lions’ or metaphorical ‘brave men’.
segments.s134-g45: کنون is the temporal adverb ‘now’. دشت روباه is a nominal phrase ‘a plain of foxes’ (روباَه modifying/qualifying دشت). بینم is the first-person-singular present ‘I see/behold’; the enclitic همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle here marking ongoing observation (‘I now see / indeed I see’). Word order places the time adverb first, then the noun phrase, then the verb.
segments.s134-g46: ز is the preposition ‘from/because of’. رزم آز is a genitival/compound notion ‘(the) greed/desire for battle’ (رزم = battle, آز = greed). کوتاه is an adjectival predicate ‘short / lacking’, and بینم همی repeats the speaker’s present observation ‘I see (it) now’ with همی marking continuing perception. Grammatically the segment states cause (from X) and the speaker’s perception of the resulting condition (کوتاه).
segments.s134-g47: ز here marks the agent in a passive construction (‘by’). مردان توران is the agent phrase ‘the men of Turan’. خنیده is a past participle ‘sung/celebrated’ (passive/predicative), and تویی is the 2nd-person copula ‘thou art’. The structure thus reads ‘You are sung of by the men of Turan.’
segments.s134-g48: Two nominal descriptors are coordinated: جهان‌جوی ‘world-seeker’ and رزمدیده ‘battle-experienced’ (veteran). و هم (‘and also’) links them, and the final تویی (‘thou art’) is the copula applying to both predicates — stylistically the copula is placed at the end, a common poetic order.
segments.s134-g65: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction meaning “(he) looked/gazed.” In classical Persian the verb can precede the subject, so نگه کرد افراسیاب = “Afrasiab looked.” از کران is a prepositional phrase “from the edge/limit.”
segments.s134-g66: چنین گفت = “thus said” (چنین = thus; گفت = said in narrative). با نامور مهتران = “to/with the renowned chiefs”; با marks accompaniment or address, and نامور is an adjective “renowned.” مهتران (here ‘great ones’/chiefs) follows the adjective pattern of older Persian.
segments.s134-g67: که‌گر is a conditional particle meaning “if.” تا شب = “until night” (تا = until, شب = night). این جنگ هم = “this battle too/also” (هم = also/even). زین نشان is an older phrasing meaning “by this sign/token”; زین is archaic/contracted (classical) and here functions like ‘by this’.
segments.s134-g68: میان = “between.” دلیران is the plural of دلیر, “the brave/heroic ones,” following the preposition. و = “and.” گردنکشان is a plural substantive meaning ‘the proud ones’ (literally ‘neck-lifters’) or rebels/proud nobles; it pairs with دلیران as the second term of the contrast.
segments.s134-g69: بماند نماند is a paired correlative meaning “whether (he) remains or does not remain” (بماند = may/remain; نماند = not remain). سواری = “a rider/horseman.” به جای = “in place / at his post.” The verb forms are classical present/subjunctive-style forms used in narrative conditionals.
segments.s134-g70: نبایست is a negative-of-obligation formula meaning “it ought not / must not be.” کردن is the light verb forming an action (here part of ‘to make/decide’). The manuscript splits بدین into ب + دین, but the intended classical particle بدین = “by/with this”; here ب is the prefix element and دین is not used in its usual sense “religion” (so the given sense list for دین does not match the usage). رزم = “battle,” رای = “counsel/decision/plan.” Altogether: “it must not be to decide this battle by such counsel.”
segments.s134-g71: بپرسید here functions as a narrative past “(he) asked” (classical usage can look like an imperative form but is narrated as past). کالکوس جنگی = name + epithet, “Kalkos the warlike one” (جنگی = warlike/fighter). کجاست = interrogative copula “where is (he)?”
segments.s134-g72: که introduces the content/reason clause (“for/that”), چندین = “several/many,” همی is a classical particle (here an intensifier: still/indeed/just), رزم شیران = “combats of the lions” where شیران figuratively means brave men/’lions’ of battle, and بخواست is the past third-person form “he desired/wished.”
segments.s134-g25: چنین (an emphatic demonstrative) precedes the noun and here means “such” (qualifying the following noun). لشکری is a collective noun “army/troops”; سرفرازان is a plural appositive describing those troops as ‘victorious/proud ones’ and stands next to لشکری without an ezāfe in poetic diction. جنگ functions as a noun 'of battle/war' that characterizes the serefrazan (i.e. ‘victorious in war’).
segments.s134-g26: همه is a distributive determiner “all/everyone” applying to the soldiers. نیزه و تیغ هندی is a coordinated noun phrase ‘spears and (Indian) swords’ with the adjective هندی following تیغ in the usual Persian order. به چنگ is a short adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning ‘into the grasp / in hand’ (the possessive is omitted, as is normal in Persian: ‘in their hands’ is implied).
segments.s134-g27: همه is the subject ‘all/everyone’. یکسر is an adverbial intensifier here meaning ‘at once’ or ‘entirely’. از جای is a prepositional phrase ‘from (their) place(s)’. برخاستند is the past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb ‘they rose/rose up’ — verb-final order is used after the fronted adverbials, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s134-g28: بسان is a comparative preposition ‘like/as’. پلنگان is the plural noun ‘leopards’, used metaphorically for sleek/agile fighters. بیاراستند is a past-tense transitive verb ‘they adorned/arrayed’ (here idiomatically ‘they armed or put themselves in battle array’); the reflexive object is omitted but understood.
segments.s134-g29: بدان‌گونه is an adverbial phrase ‘in that manner / thus’ placed before the verb for emphasis. شد is the past-tense verb ‘became / was’. گیو is the subject (proper name) and در کارزار is a locative phrase ‘in the fight / on the battlefield’ specifying where he became so; the verb preceding the subject is a poetic inversion.
segments.s134-g30: چو is a simile marker ‘like/as’. شیری here means ‘a lion’ (leonine) — the -ی ending is an adjectival/nominal form used poetically. که introduces a relative clause ‘that/who’. گم‌کرده is a perfect participle ‘having lost’ and is followed by باشد (the classical subjunctive/present form of ‘to be’ used as an auxiliary) to produce the sense ‘that has lost’ or ‘who had lost’ — شکار is the object ‘prey/quarry’. This is an instance of classical poetic auxiliary use (گم‌کرده باشد = ‘that has lost’).
segments.s134-g31: پس و پیش is an idiomatic pair meaning ‘back and forth / to and fro’ (directional adverbial). هر سو means ‘every side’ (هر = each/every, سو = side/direction). همی is a continuous/aspectual particle meaning ‘kept on / was continuously’. کوفت is the past verb ‘struck/was striking’ and گرز is the direct object ‘mace/club’. Word order places the adverbials up front and the verb with its object after, typical of heroic poetic narration.
segments.s134-g32: دو تا کرد is an idiomatic sequence where the numeral phrase دو تا plus کرد indicates ‘(he) made/delivered two (blows)’ or ‘struck twice’ (کرد is the light verb ‘made/did’). بسیار is an intensifier ‘very’ and بالای برز is a locative/adverbial phrase ‘high up / on high’ (بالای = atop/above, برز = height/high). Together they qualify the manner and height of the blows.
segments.s134-g129: چنان (preverbal adverb) = 'thus/so', setting the manner. برگرفتند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb (برگرفتن) meaning here 'rose/moved off' (in classical Persian, برگرفتن از جای = to get up/leave one's place). لشکر is the subject coming after the verb (typical Persian flexible order). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from place' (ز = poetic 'from', جای = 'place').
segments.s134-g130: که introduces a result/clause ('so that/that'). پیدا نیامد is an impersonal/predicative combination: پیدا (visible) + نیامد (did not appear) = 'did not appear/was not visible'. همی is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle adding emphasis/continuity (here an intensifier: 'at all/indeed'). سر ز پای is a frozen idiomatic juxtaposition 'head from foot' meaning distinguishing head from foot; ز = 'from'.
segments.s134-g131: بکشتند is past, 3rd-person-plural of کشتن 'they killed' (active). چندان here quantifies the extent ('so many/to that extent'). ز + جنگ‌آوران is the partitive/ablative expression 'of/from the warriors' (z marks the group from which many were killed).
segments.s134-g132: که introduces the result clause 'so that'. شد is the past of شدن 'became'. خاک لعل is a noun phrase where لعل ('ruby') qualifies خاک ('earth/dust') = 'ruby earth' (metaphoric). از کران تا کران is a classical circumstantial phrase 'from edge to edge' (از = from; تا = 'as far as' here; کران = edge/limit).
segments.s134-g133: فگنده is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'thrown/scattered' (passive sense). چو = 'like/as' introduces a simile. پیلان (plural of پیل) 'elephants' is the object of comparison. به هر جای بر is a dispersed locative phrase: به = 'to/into', هر جای = 'every place', بر = a prepositional/locative particle 'upon/over' giving the sense 'all over every place'.
segments.s134-g134: This is a correlative construction (چه ... چه ...) meaning 'whether ... or ...'. چه با تن (with body) چه بی‌تن (without body) lists the two alternatives. جدا کرده سر is a verbal-nominal cluster: جدا (apart) + کرده (perfect/participle of کردن: 'having done') + سر (head) — together 'having cut/separated heads apart' (i.e., they severed heads, whether attached to bodies or not). Note the verb-like sense is carried by کرده, with سر the object.
segments.s134-g135: به آوردگه is a locative prepositional phrase 'to/at the place of bringing/assembly' (آوردگه = 'place of arrival/assembly'). جای گشتن is a noun phrase where جای ('place/room') is followed by the infinitive گشتن ('to go/turn'), producing 'a place to go/move'. نماند is past of ماندن 'remain' negated here: 'there remained no ...' — so the whole means 'there remained no place to move at the assembly-ground.'
segments.s134-g136: سپه را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker (here topicalizing 'the army'). ره برگذشتن is a compound nominal phrase: ره ('way') + برگذشتن ('to pass over/pass by') functioning as 'a way of passing'. نماند again 'remained not/was no longer left' — 'the army had no road/path left to pass.'
segments.s134-g49: سنان را is a noun + definite-object marker (را) marking 'sanan' as the direct object. به تندی is an adverbial phrase where به + noun forms an adverbial 'with haste' (here functioning like 'quickly'). یکی modifies the following verb and here means 'to one side' / 'to one (direction)'. برگرای is an older/poetic imperative 'turn (toward)' directed at the addressee, so the whole segment is an imperative clause 'Turn the spear quickly to one side.'
segments.s134-g50: برو is the familiar imperative 'go' and زود is an adverbial intensifier 'quickly/at once.' زیشان is a contracted prepositional phrase meaning 'from them / against them' (objects of the forthcoming action). بپرداز is an imperative from پرداختن used here in the sense 'set upon / attack / deal with.' جای functions as a noun 'place' meaning 'their place' or 'the position to be taken' and completes the command 'attack them, take their place.'
segments.s134-g51: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when' or 'if'). پیروزگر is a predicate noun 'victor / triumphant one.' باشی is the second-person present/subjunctive form 'you be' (here used in the protasis 'when/if you are'). ایران تراست is the main clause: تراست is ترا + است, a poetic fusion meaning 'is to you' or simply 'belongs to you.'
segments.s134-g52: This is a nominal sentence listing possessions: تن پیل 'the bodies (or flesh) of elephants' and چنگال شیران 'the claws of lions.' و links the two noun phrases; تراست repeats the same predication 'are yours.' Word order is noun phrase + predicate 'تراست', the typical Persian copular structure in poetry.
segments.s134-g53: چو introduces 'when.' پیران here grammatically functions as a plural noun 'the elders' (in epic it can also be a name in other contexts); ز + افراسیاب is the source phrase 'from Afrasiab.' این is the pronoun 'this' (the preceding news), and شنید is the simple past 'heard.' The line thus reads 'When the elders heard this from Afrasiab.'
segments.s134-g54: Another 'چو' clause 'when'; از marks cause or source 'because of / from.' باد آتش is a poetic genitive 'the wind of fire' (literally 'wind of fire'), i.e. a violent gust or metaphor for sudden fury. دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his), and بردمید is a past verbal compound meaning 'was carried off / seized' — together: 'when, because of the wind of fire, his heart was carried away.'
segments.s134-g55: بسیچید is a past/epic perfect tense 'he mustered / assembled' (third-person). با introduces accompaniment or composition 'with / consisting of.' نامور modifies the following number as an epithet 'renowned.' ده‌هزار is a compound numeral 'ten thousand' often used as a troop-count in epic Persian.
segments.s134-g56: ز here means 'from' or 'out of' (a poetic source marker). ترکان is the source group 'the Turks.' دلیران is a plural adjective/noun 'the brave ones' describing those troops, and خنجرگذار is an agent noun 'dagger‑attacker / dagger‑wielder' — an appositive describing the kind of warriors mustered. The phrase specifies 'he raised ten thousand famed warriors from the Turks, brave dagger‑wielders.'
segments.s134-g105: به الکوس بر زد یکی بانگ تند is a compact clause in which به + proper name marks the target/direction ('towards/at Alkūs'), and بر زد is the past of زدن used with بانگ to mean 'gave/let out' (i.e., 'uttered'). یکی qualifies بانگ as 'a single/certain' cry and تند is an adjective modifying بانگ ('sharp/fierce'); word order is topical (target first) then verbal phrase.
segments.s134-g106: کجا here functions adverbially ('whereupon' / 'so that'), introducing the result clause. دست شد سست is a simple past copular idea 'the hand became slack' (شد = become). و connects the second predicate شمشیر کند where کند is predicative and here means 'became slow/blunt' (i.e. the sword lost sharpness).
segments.s134-g107: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. Subject (الکوس) is followed by the object phrase آوای رستم ('the voice of Rostam') and then the past verb شنید ('heard'), i.e. S + O + V word order typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s134-g108: دلش گفتی از پوست آمد پدید contains an idiomatic, slightly poetic parenthetical گفتی meaning 'so to speak / as if' (here marking a simile/metaphor). دلش = 'his heart', with enclitic ش. از پوست آمد پدید literally 'came forth from the skin' (آمد + پدید = 'appeared/emerged'); the phrase conveys an image of the heart leaping or appearing at the surface. Note: گفتی is a discourse particle here (archaic), not the noun 'conversation'.
segments.s134-g109: به زین اندر آمد is literal 'he came/entered onto the saddle' (به + زین marks movement onto the saddle; اندر = 'into/within'). به کردار باد is a simile 'in the manner of the wind' (کردار = 'manner/deed', باد = 'wind'), so the clause as a whole says he mounted and moved off like the wind.
segments.s134-g110: ز مردی بدل در نیامدش یاد is a negative experiential clause: ز + مردی بدل = 'any retaliation/revenge against the man' as the theme, and در نیامدش یاد = 'no memory/thought came to him' (نیامدش = 'did not come to him', یاد = 'thought/memory'). The whole means he felt no thought of taking revenge.
segments.s134-g111: بدو گفت رستم is an instance of the dative/topic first form: بدو = 'to him', followed by گفت with the subject name after the verb (رستم) = 'Rostam said to him'. که introduces the content clause. چنگال شیر = 'the claw(s) of a lion' (a genitive phrase), the object of the implied verb in the content clause.
segments.s134-g112: نپیموده‌ای is a negative perfect/experiential form 'you have not experienced/traversed' (here idiomatically 'you have not suffered/felt' the lion's claws). زان = 'therefore/for that reason' links cause and effect. شدستی is an archaic second-person past/copular form meaning 'you became' (grammatically the past result), and دلیر is the predicate adjective 'brave'. Together: 'you have not felt a lion's claws; therefore you became brave.'
segments.s134-g113: The clause names the person (زواره) then gives a short state and action: 'به درد' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in pain' and appears after the name. 'از بر زین نشست' is a classical sequence meaning 'dismounted from the saddle' — از + بر (literally 'from off upon') + زین 'saddle' followed by the past verb نشست 'sat/dismounted'. The verb is simple past third-person; subject is the preceding proper name.
segments.s134-g114: 'پر از خون تن' is a nominal phrase 'a body full of blood' where پر (here 'full (of)') takes از + noun (خون). تن 'body' is the noun being qualified. The conjunction و links the next phrase 'تیغ مانده به دست': مانده is a perfect participle meaning 'remaining/left (behind)'; 'به دست' here functions like 'in the (his) hand' (possessive pronoun is omitted but understood from context).
segments.s134-g115: The verb برآویخت (a classical/poetic past) precedes the subject name (الکوس) — inversion common in epic style — and means 'sprang up/rose'. با پیلتن 'with Pilten' is an accompaniment prepositional phrase: both rise or act together.
segments.s134-g116: بپوشید is a past-tense verb here meaning 'he covered' (sense: to cover). The object کفن 'shroud' appears; بر زین gives the location 'on the saddle'. توزی is an archaic/poetic form attached in classical Persian (here acting to mark possession or an archaic verbal/compatibility form) — the line means 'he covered the saddle with a shroud'. Word order (verb before object, prepositional phrase after) is typical of the verse style.
segments.s134-g117: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'someone'. The verb زد 'struck/thrust' takes the prepositional phrase بر کمربند اوی 'at/on his belt' (اوی is an archaic possessive 'his'). The order is Subject (indefinite) + object + verb, a flexible arrangement in epic Persian.
segments.s134-g118: ز دامن 'from the hem' (ز = from) starts the clause; نشد is the past of شدن meaning 'did not become / did not happen' (negative sense here implied by context), so together it says 'did not come off / did not become distant'. دور پیوند اوی 'the fastening/tie of his (garment) did not become distant' — پیوند 'bond/fastening' and اوی again marks possession. The overall meaning: the belt's fastening did not give way from the hem.
segments.s134-g119: تهمتن (an epithet/name) is the agent. Here یکی qualifies نیزه as 'one (single) spear' (numeral), so the phrase means 'Tahmatan struck with a single spear'. زد is past 'struck'; بر + برش functions as 'at/on him' — in classical usage بر + pronominal suffix (ش) yields 'on him/his body' (so برش = 'on him / on his part').
segments.s134-g120: This clause describes the result: به خون جگر غرقه شد مغفرش. غرقه شد is a passive/resultative phrase 'became drenched/immersed'. خون جگر 'the blood of (his) liver' is the instrument/substance soaking it. مغفرش is 'his covering/veil/helmet' (مغفر = covering; ـش = his). The preposition به here introduces the material/agent with which something is drenched ('with/in').
segments.s134-g89: زواره is a proper name (subject). برآویخت is past tense (preterite) meaning 'rose up/attacked' and takes the following prepositional phrase. با او به هم is a clustered prepositional expression: با او ('with/at him') + به هم ('together/against one another'), so the sequence says he rushed at/engaged that other person together (i.e. they grappled). Word order is subject + verb + prepositional complement.
segments.s134-g90: چو is a simile marker 'like/as'. Noun + adjective order is noun first then modifier: پیل سرافراز = 'an elephant lofty/proud'. و simply links the two similes. شیر دژم = 'a lion ḏajam' where دژم is an adjective meaning 'grim/sullen'; the whole clause compares their meeting to these two fierce beasts.
segments.s134-g91: سناندار is an agent noun 'spear‑bearer' (subject). نیزه به دو نیم کرد is a verbal phrase: به دو نیم ('into two halves') is the resultive phrase and کرد (preterite 'did/made') is the main verb — here 'broke/split (the spear) into two'. Word order: subject + object phrase + verbal action.
segments.s134-g92: دل شیر is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the lion's heart' (noun + possessor). چنگی is a deverbal noun meaning 'a gripping/seizing (action)' and here functions as the thing that affects the heart. پر از بیم is an adjectival phrase 'full of fear' (پر + از + noun). کرد is causative/past 'caused/made' — together: 'caused the lion's heart to be gripped/full of fear.'
segments.s134-g93: بزنPast (بزد) 'he struck' is a simple past verb; دست و تیغ lists two instruments/objects 'hand and sword'. از میان ('from the middle/between') modifies برکشید (past 'drew/raised'), so the clause reads: 'he struck (with) his hand and drew the sword out from between (them).' Note the sequence of verbs in past narrative.
segments.s134-g94: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گرد here is best read as 'dust/whirl (of dust)' and سران is 'the chiefs/heads'; شد is past 'became' and زمین ناپدید is 'the ground (became) unseen/vanished.' Grammatically this is a verbless-result clause with شد as the copular verb: 'from the chiefs' dust the ground became invisible/hidden.'
segments.s134-g95: ز introduces an ablative/cause 'from/because of'. کین‌آوران is the agent/group 'the avengers'. تیغ بر هم شکست is a compact verbal phrase: تیغ (swords) + بر هم (against one another) + شکست (past 'broke/was broken' or 'clashed and broke') — meaning the swords struck each other and were broken/clashed together.
segments.s134-g96: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'. گرز is 'mace/club' and بردند is past plural 'they carried/went with'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and باد دست 'the hand of the wind' is a poetic image for a swift, sweeping movement. The clause says they bore maces toward (him) with the swiftness/force of the wind's hand.
segments.s134-g17: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause; در is the preposition 'in/at' governing جوشن 'cuirass, coat of mail'. افراسیابش is the proper name افراسیاب with the third-person enclitic -ش (his/him) attached — a common poetic clitic marking the noun; بدید is the older past form (بـ + دید) 'saw' (3rd singular). Word order here is [when] + [prepositional phrase] + verb.
segments.s134-g18: تو گفتی is a simple past 2nd‑person clause 'you said' (تو + گفتی). که introduces reported speech ('that'). هوش 'awareness/sense' is the subject of the reported clause; از دلش is a prepositional phrase 'from his heart' where ش is the 3rd‑person possessive clitic on دل. بر پرید is a compound past verb (بر + پرید) meaning literally 'flew up/away' and idiomatically 'lost his wits' — here used in simple past (3rd singular) to describe the mental shock.
segments.s134-g19: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'of', introducing a list of body‑parts: چنگ (claw), و (and) بر (here another listed part), بازو (arm), یال (mane). The coordinating و links the list; او at the end is the 3rd‑person clitic 'his', referring back to the creature whose parts are being named. This is a poetic enumeration of trophies/parts.
segments.s134-g20: به گردن gives the target 'to/around the neck' (به = to/onto, گردن = neck). برآورده is the participial/past form of برآوردن used here with the sense 'brought/put' or 'hung' (poetic past), and the noun phrase گوپال او ('Gopāl' with the 3rd‑person clitic او = 'his') is the object placed at the neck; word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (target phrase + verb + object).
segments.s134-g21: چو is the simile marker 'like/as' introducing a list of examples. Two proper names follow (طوس, گودرز) joined by و 'and'; the substantive نیزه‌گذار comes after them and functions as a predicate/noun phrase describing those names (there is no copula — the verb 'are' is omitted as is normal in Persian poetic lists). Word order is [چو NAME و چو NAME + predicate].
segments.s134-g22: Another simile clause introduced by چو/چون 'like/as' listing two names (گرگین and گیو). The phrase گرد و سوار is a coordinated description ('X and Y') attached to those names — in epic usage this contrasts types of fighters (commonly 'infantry and cavalry'). The line omits any linking verb; چون here is just a variant spelling of چو. The precise meaning of گرد is not available in the provided senses, so it is treated as part of the fixed phrase گرد و سوار.
segments.s134-g23: چو / چون 'like/as' again introduces names used as examples. بهرام is a proper name; زنگه شادروان is a name plus an epithet/title (شادروان) attached to it. There is no copula; the whole group is presented as comparable examples in a catalog of heroes. The provided senses for زنگه and شادروان do not match their use here as a personal name plus epithet, so they are marked unavailable.
segments.s134-g24: The pattern repeats: چو introduces two names (فرهاد و برزین) and the plural noun جنگ‌آوران 'warriors' follows to describe them. Note the plural noun explicitly marks them as fighters here (unlike the singular predicate in the earlier segment); again the copula is omitted in poetic Persian.
segments.s134-g73: به مستی is an adverbial phrase 'in/through drunkenness' where به functions adverbially ('by/with'); همی is an archaic continuous/habitual particle placed before the verb complex; گیو را is the definite object (گیو + را) and خواستی is the simple past (2nd person) of خواستن used here as 'you sought/attempted' (the subject 'you' is implied). Word order is adverb — continuous particle — object — verb (poetic inversion is allowed).
segments.s134-g74: همه modifies جنگ ('the whole/entire fight/force'); با رستم is a prepositional phrase 'against/with Rostam' marking the adversary; آراستی is a past tense (2nd person) verb 'you arrayed/armed/arranged'. The clause follows the typical SOV pattern of classical Persian with the verb at the end.
segments.s134-g75: همیشه is an adverb 'always'; از ایران indicates the domain/source 'in/from Iran' (here 'among the Iranians'); بدی یاد is a noun phrase 'the memory/remembrance of evil' and اوی is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to یاد. The sequence places adverb and locative phrase before the noun phrase that carries the possessive.
segments.s134-g76: کجا شد is an interrogative/inquisitive past construction 'where did (it) become/go'; چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'such/so'; آتش و باد اوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'the fire and wind of him' (اوی = 'his'). The verb شد comes before the modifying noun phrase because of the poetic/questioning word order.
segments.s134-g77: به الکوس رفت is a simple motion clause 'went to Alkous' with به marking direction; آگهی زین سخن is an instrumental/genitive phrase 'news/information of/from this speech' — here زین (archaic) functions like 'از این' = 'of/from this'. The noun آگهی ('news/information') sits with the following genitive phrase.
segments.s134-g78: که introduces a content clause 'that/what' (here asking indirectly); سالار توران is the subject phrase 'the commander of Turan'; چه is an interrogative 'what' and افگند is the past 'he cast/threw/caused to fall'; بن is the noun 'base/foundation' (object of افگند). The clause means 'what the Turanian commander cast at/down on the base.'
segments.s134-g79: برانگیخت is a past (3rd person) verb 'he roused/awoke/raised'; الکوس immediately after the verb is the agent (poetic inversion places the subject after the verb); شبرنگ را is the definite object marked by را. In classical Persian poetry such verb–subject–object ordering is common.
segments.s134-g80: به خون شسته is an adjectival/participial phrase 'washed/soaked in blood' where به is used instrumentally/adverbially; بد بی‌گمان is an adverbial cluster 'undoubtedly/indeed (evil/terrible)' qualifying the action; چنگ را is a definite object (here 'چنگ' is used as 'claw/talon' or possibly a name) marked by را. Grammatically شسته (= 'washed') functions as verb/participle governing چنگ را, so the clause says someone soaked/washed the cheng in blood.
segments.s134-g1: چنین گفت is a fixed poetic phrasing meaning “thus said”; گفت (past of گفتن) precedes or accompanies the speaker’s name as is common in epic Persian. پس = “then” is a temporal adverb, and گیو با پهلوان uses با to mark the addressee (‘to/with the champion’), so the whole chunk means ‘Thus (thus) said, then Giv to the champion.’
segments.s134-g2: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/relative opening (classical 'که ای' = 'O you who...'); نازش is ناز + -ش (possessive) = ‘his/its pride’ and modifies the following nouns; شهریار و گوان are coordinated nouns ‘king and ...’ (gوان is an archaic word — contextually ‘warriors/men’). The clause addresses the addressee as ‘O (you) the pride of the shah and of the warriors.’
segments.s134-g3: شوم + بگیرم: شوم is an optative/first‑person form (‘may I / I will’) combined with the present‑subjunctive/intentional بگیرم ‘I take’ to express intention (‘I will set out’). به marks direction (‘toward/to Afrasiab’). Word order places the verbal complex before the directional phrase, typical in poetry.
segments.s134-g4: نمانم is negative subjunctive/intentional (‘I will not remain’). که introduces the subordinate clause (‘that/so that/he might’); آید is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive ‘he may come’ (used for future/hypothetical). بدین روی آب = ‘to this/that side/surface of the water’ (ب = to, + دین/این = ‘this’ in the manuscript segmentation, روی آب = ‘the surface/side of the water’).
segments.s134-g5: سر پل بگیرم: سر = ‘head/top’ + پل ‘bridge’, and بگیرم (first‑person subjunctive/intentional) ‘I will take/occupy’ — ‘I will take up position at the head of the bridge.’ بدان بدگمان uses دان as ‘consider/hold’ and the adjective بدگمان ‘suspicious’ to mean ‘regard him as suspicious.’
segments.s134-g6: بدارمش is a verb + object clitic (دار + م + ش) meaning ‘I will keep/detain him’ (literally can mean ‘hang him’ in other contexts, but here idiomatically ‘hold/detain’); ازان سوی پل = ‘on that side of the bridge’ (ازان = ‘from that/of that’, سوی = ‘side/toward’); یک زمان = ‘for a while’ (temporary duration).
segments.s134-g7: بدان is a connective adverb (‘thereupon/with that’) linking to the previous action; تا introduces a purpose/temporal clause ‘until/so that’. بپوشند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive of پوشیدن ‘they may clothe/arm’; گردان سلیح = ‘the troop (گردان) with arms/armor (سلیح)’, object follows the verb as usual.
segments.s134-g8: که introduces the result clause; بر ما = ‘upon us’; سرآمد here expresses the notion of an end/conclusion coming upon them (‘the end befell us’), and نشاط و مزیح are the nouns ‘gaiety/delight and jest/mockery’. The whole says that as they armed, their merriment ceased.
segments.s134-g33: رمیدند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they fled/ran away'. ازو is the poetic fused form of 'از او' (= 'from him'), placed before the subject. رزمسازان is a plural agent noun 'war-makers/warriors' modifying چین, so the whole phrase means 'the Chinese warriors fled from him.' Word order (prepositional phrase + verb + subject) is idiomatic in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s134-g34: بشد is the past/heavy-form 'became/was' (it happened/it became) used as a copula here. خیره is adjectival 'staring/astonished' and functions as the predicate complement. سالار توران زمین is a noun phrase 'the commander/chief of the land of Turan' (year/land-name postmodifies سالار). The normal order in modern Persian would be سالار توران زمین خیره شد; the poem places the verb earlier for rhythm.
segments.s134-g35: ز (poetic for از) here introduces the cause/object of fear: 'of/from Rostam'. بترسید is past 3rd-person-singular 'he feared/was afraid'. The name افراسیاب follows in the line's word order but is the subject: 'Afrasiab feared Rostam.'
segments.s134-g36: نکرد is past negative 'did not do'. ایچ is an emphatic negative 'anything/at all' ('he did nothing / not at all'). بر here has the sense 'toward/for' and combines with the infinitive phrase کینه جستن 'to seek (a) grudge/revenge'. شتاب is a noun 'haste' — the whole clause means 'he did not at all make haste to seek revenge.' Note the infinitive جستن is used like an English 'to seek'.
segments.s134-g37: پس is a simple connective 'then/thereupon'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. اندر means 'in/into' and with راند (past 'drove/put to flight' or 'pressed') gives 'drove in' or 'pressed in'. همی is the frequent poetic particle that adds continuity/emphasis ('just/indeed'). گرم is adverbial here 'fiercely/with ardor' (not literal temperature).
segments.s134-g38: گوان را is treated as the direct/topic object ('the like/those ones' or 'the men' in context) marked by the object marker را. ز لشکر 'from the ranks/army' gives source. همی (again) is the poetic continuative particle. خواند here is the verb 'called/summoned' (sense 'to call/summon'), and نرم is an adverb 'softly/gently'. Together: 'he gently summoned them from the army.'
segments.s134-g39: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' indicating origin (here 'from Turan'). فراوان is an adverb 'in great number/abundantly'. سران is the plural 'heads/leaders/chiefs' (the direct affected party). کشته شد is a passive construction 'were killed' (کشته = slain, شد = became/was). The line states that many Turanian chiefs were slain.
segments.s134-g40: This is an elliptic, proverbial-sounding clause: سر بخت here centers on 'بخت' (fortune/fate) with سر placed before it for emphasis ('the head/turn of fortune' → 'their fortune'). گردنکشان is an epithet 'neck‑breakers' (= proud/rebellious ones). گشته شد is a compound verbal phrase 'was turned/has become' (perfect/state result). Together the sense is 'the proud rebels' luck/fortune was turned/overturned' (i.e., their fortunes ran out).
